text:
The rapid convergence of neurotechnology and artificial intelligence has enabled unprecedented access to and modulation of the human brain, prompting urgent international discussions on the protection of "neurorights." While various governmental and intergovernmental bodies—including UNESCO and the Council of Europe—are actively exploring new legal frameworks, the scholarly debate remains fragmented by conflicting philosophical assumptions and inconsistent legal interpretations. This multidisciplinary paper, authored by a diverse group of experts in law, philosophy, ethics, and neuroscience, seeks to provide a rigorous conceptual and legal mapping of the three pillars of neuroprotection: mental privacy, mental integrity, and cognitive liberty. 

We first define these concepts through a minimalist lens, distinguishing mental integrity as protection against unauthorized interference, mental privacy as a safeguard against non-consensual access to mental data, and cognitive liberty as the right to mental self-determination. We then analyze how these concepts are currently anchored—or could be integrated—within established human rights instruments, such as the European Convention on Human Rights and the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights. By clarifying whether existing legal protections are sufficient or require adaptive interpretation, this work provides a foundational framework to harmonize international policy and ensure robust legal safeguards for the mind in the face of emerging technological capabilities.
